items.q: What does an NMR chemical shift (δ, in ppm) actually measure?
items.correct: The position of a peak RELATIVE to TMS (tetramethylsilane, δ = 0), normalised by the spectrometer frequency.
items.misconception: Chemical shift is the absolute resonance frequency of the nucleus in Hz.
items.random_wrong: Chemical shift is the mass of the molecule divided by 1000.
items.why: Students see ppm and think it's an absolute scale, not realising it's a frequency-independent ratio against a reference.

items.q: Why does the colour of a transition metal complex change when ligands are exchanged?
items.correct: Different ligands cause different amounts of d-orbital splitting, so different wavelengths of light are absorbed.
items.misconception: The colour changes because the metal ion itself changes oxidation state.
items.random_wrong: The colour changes because the new ligand is itself coloured.
items.why: Students assume colour comes from the ligand or from a redox change; they miss that the same metal ion gives different colours depending on which ligands are bound.

items.q: Which of these is a molecule of a compound?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: H₂O. “O₂” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many atoms in one molecule of methane, CH₄?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
